summary: DHH highlights the irony of Zoom, a company built on remote collaboration tools, mandating its employees return to the office. He argues this is part of a broader opportunistic squeeze by big tech companies who are exploiting a weakened job market and mass layoffs to force return-to-office policies. He notes that workers currently lack leverage to resist since hiring has cooled outside of AI. Despite the trend, he predicts remote work is too compelling to disappear and that companies committed to it will benefit from an oversupply of eager applicants.
key_points: Zoom mandating return-to-office is deeply ironic given its entire business is selling remote collaboration
Companies are exploiting a weak job market and post-layoff fear to push RTO policies they couldn't enforce during the talent war
The return-to-office wave has reached critical mass, giving individual companies safety in numbers to follow suit
Remote-first companies like Shopify, Automattic, and 37signals will benefit from increasingly oversubscribed job openings
Remote work's long-term trend remains upward despite the current corporate backlash
money_quotes: The company that literally lives to sell us all on the wonders of remote collaboration wants its own people back into the office again.
Which I guess is just a regression to the mean of productivity tool makers failing to believe their own marketing, but it's a bit sad none the less.
This is an opportunistic squeeze, because what's most tech workers supposed to do?
The option of simply hopping to another company has been severely limited.
So this is happening now because it can happen now.
I just hope that people who really did enjoy that remote work-lifestyle don't forget what it was like.
Remote work might be receding, but it's simply too compelling an option for too many to ever disappear.
The long-term trend line ain't going anywhere.
key_insight: Big tech's return-to-office push is an opportunistic power play enabled by a weak job market, not a genuine indictment of remote work's effectiveness.
